enrichment.short_description: Open-source integrated library system (ILS) used by libraries and consortia worldwide for cataloging, circulation, and public search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Evergreen is a free, open-source integrated library system (ILS) originally built by the Georgia Public Library Service in 2004 for PINES, a statewide resource-sharing consortium. It handles cataloging, circulation, acquisitions, serials, and a public search catalog (OPAC), and is released under the GNU General Public License.

The software is now stewarded through the Software Freedom Conservancy with an independent community oversight board, and it powers statewide and regional library consortia as well as individual libraries in roughly 1,800 installations worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Evergreen is aimed at public, academic, and consortial libraries that need to manage large, multi-branch or multi-library catalogs without paying per-seat ILS licensing fees. It's particularly suited to statewide or regional resource-sharing networks where many libraries need to share a single catalog and circulation pool.

features.title: Circulation & patron management
features.description: Checkouts, holds, renewals, fines, and patron records across single libraries or large multi-branch consortia.

features.title: Public search catalog (OPAC)
features.description: A web-based public catalog that lets patrons search, place holds, and manage their own accounts.

features.title: Consortial resource sharing
features.description: Built from the ground up to let many independent libraries share one catalog and circulate items between branches.

features.title: Cataloging
features.description: MARC-based bibliographic and authority record management for library staff.

features.title: Acquisitions & serials
features.description: Modules for ordering materials and tracking serial subscriptions alongside circulation data.

features.title: Reporting
features.description: Built-in reporting tools for circulation, collection, and usage statistics.

pros: No licensing fees — free and open source under the GPL
Proven at scale across roughly 1,800 library installations
Purpose-built for multi-library consortial resource sharing
Governed by an independent community under the Software Freedom Conservancy
cons: Requires server administration and technical expertise to self-host
Initial setup and data migration from legacy systems can be complex
Smaller commercial support ecosystem than proprietary ILS vendors

faqs.question: Is Evergreen free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Evergreen is free, open-source software released under the GNU General Public License; there are no licensing fees to run it.

faqs.question: Who maintains Evergreen?
faqs.answer: It is maintained by an open community of libraries and developers under the Software Freedom Conservancy, with commercial support available from partners such as Equinox Software.

faqs.question: Can independent libraries use Evergreen, or is it only for consortia?
faqs.answer: Both. Evergreen was designed for multi-library consortia but is also used by individual, standalone libraries.

faqs.question: Does Evergreen support MARC cataloging?
faqs.answer: Yes, cataloging in Evergreen is based on the MARC bibliographic and authority record standards used throughout the library field.

use_cases.title: Statewide library consortia
use_cases.description: Share a single catalog and circulation pool across dozens or hundreds of member libraries.

use_cases.title: Public library systems
use_cases.description: Manage cataloging, circulation, and patron accounts for multi-branch public library systems.

use_cases.title: Academic library networks
use_cases.description: Run a shared ILS across affiliated academic or special libraries.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Evergreen is a free, open-source ILS for library cataloging, circulation, and consortial resource sharing, used in roughly 1,800 libraries worldwide.

enrichment.short_description: The world's first free and open-source integrated library system, used by libraries globally for cataloging, circulation, and public search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koha is a fully-featured, scalable library management system billed by the Koha community as the world's first free and open-source ILS, originally created in New Zealand in 1999. It's free to download, install, and modify, with development backed by libraries of all sizes, volunteer contributors, and a network of commercial support companies worldwide.
The software is responsive across devices and covers the core functions a library needs: cataloging, circulation, acquisitions, serials, and a public-facing search catalog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Koha suits public, school, academic, and special libraries of any size that want a mature, actively maintained ILS without recurring software licensing costs. Because paid support and hosting are available from many independent vendors, it also works for libraries that want commercial help without vendor lock-in.

features.title: Cataloging
features.description: MARC21 and UNIMARC-based bibliographic and authority record management.

features.title: Circulation & patron management
features.description: Checkouts, holds, renewals, and patron records across single or multi-branch libraries.

features.title: Public catalog (OPAC)
features.description: A responsive, web-based public search interface for patrons.

features.title: Acquisitions & serials
features.description: Tools for ordering materials and managing serial subscriptions.

features.title: Multi-library support
features.description: Runs single independent libraries or multi-branch and consortial setups.

features.title: SIP2 & self-checkout support
features.description: Integrates with self-checkout stations and third-party patron systems via SIP2.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing fees
Mature project with over two decades of production use
Large global community plus many commercial support and hosting providers
Highly configurable for libraries of any size
cons: Self-hosting requires server administration and Perl/technical expertise
Initial configuration and data migration can be involved
Staff face a learning curve compared to simpler consumer tools

faqs.question: Is Koha really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koha is free and open-source software; there is no license fee to download, install, or modify it.

faqs.question: Can I get paid support for Koha?
faqs.answer: Yes, numerous independent companies worldwide offer paid hosting, migration, and support services for Koha.

faqs.question: What library types use Koha?
faqs.answer: Public, school, academic, and special libraries of all sizes use Koha, from single-branch libraries to larger consortia.

faqs.question: Does Koha support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Koha has a multilingual community and interface, though exact language availability depends on the version and translation coverage.

use_cases.title: Public libraries
use_cases.description: Run cataloging, circulation, and patron self-service for one or many branches.

use_cases.title: School and academic libraries
use_cases.description: Manage collections and circulation for K-12 or higher-education library collections.

use_cases.title: Special and consortial libraries
use_cases.description: Support shared cataloging across affiliated or member libraries.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koha is the world's first free, open-source integrated library system, offering cataloging, circulation, and OPAC search for libraries of any size.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, Invenio-based integrated library system built in Switzerland for library cataloging, circulation, and multilingual public search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RERO ILS is a next-generation, open-source integrated library system developed in Switzerland by RERO+ in collaboration with the Catholic University of Louvain. Under heavy development since 2017 and released as v1.0 at the end of 2020, it is built on the Invenio framework originally created at CERN and is distributed under the GNU Affero General Public License, with source code hosted publicly on GitHub.

It manages document acquisition, circulation, and cataloging for library networks or independent libraries, and replaces traditional MARC field codes with natural-language labels. It also integrates with authority networks such as IdRef, GND, and RERO RAMEAU, and uses a multilingual authority file based on VIAF.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: RERO ILS was built for the RERO+ network of libraries across Switzerland (58 libraries went live on it in July 2021) and suits library networks — particularly multilingual or cantonal/regional ones — that want a modern, API-driven ILS and are comfortable running a Python/Invenio-based stack.

features.title: Invenio-based architecture
features.description: Built on the Invenio digital library framework originally developed at CERN.

features.title: Natural-language cataloging
features.description: Replaces raw MARC field codes with readable, natural-language field labels for catalogers.

features.title: Multilingual authority file
features.description: A shared authority file based on VIAF, with community-driven translation.

features.title: Authority network integration
features.description: Connects to external authority sources including IdRef, GND, and RERO RAMEAU.

features.title: Circulation & acquisitions
features.description: Standard library workflows for circulation, holds, and document acquisition.

features.title: Public search interface
features.description: A public-facing catalog for patrons to search the collection.

pros: Modern architecture built on CERN's actively developed Invenio framework
Strong multilingual and multi-authority-network integration
Fully open source under the AGPL with public source code
In active production use across dozens of Swiss libraries
cons: Built primarily around the RERO+ network's workflows and language context
Smaller community and documentation base than Koha or Evergreen
Requires familiarity with the Python/Invenio stack to self-host

faqs.question: Is RERO ILS open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is released under the GNU Affero General Public License with source code publicly available on GitHub.

faqs.question: What is RERO ILS built on?
faqs.answer: It's built on the Invenio framework originally developed at CERN.

faqs.question: Who uses RERO ILS?
faqs.answer: It was developed for and is used by RERO+, a network of libraries across Switzerland; 58 libraries migrated to it in 2021.

faqs.question: Can other libraries outside RERO+ adopt it?
faqs.answer: The source code is public on GitHub, so technically any organization can self-host it, though it was designed around RERO+'s network needs.

use_cases.title: Regional/cantonal library networks
use_cases.description: Run a shared, multilingual ILS across a network of affiliated libraries.

use_cases.title: Academic library networks
use_cases.description: Support cataloging and circulation for universities partnering on shared infrastructure.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RERO ILS is an open-source, Invenio-based library system from Switzerland's RERO+ network, offering multilingual cataloging and circulation.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, high-performance PHP e-commerce framework for building shops, marketplaces, and B2B platforms on Laravel, Symfony, or TYPO3.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Aimeos is a cloud-native, API-first PHP e-commerce framework developed by a worldwide community since 2015 and available in over 30 languages. It's designed for flexibility, performance, and scale — handling catalogs from a handful of items up to a billion — and ships as native packages for Laravel, Symfony, and TYPO3.

It offers JSON REST and GraphQL APIs, support for multiple vendors, sales channels, and warehouses, subscription and bundle product types, and integration with 100+ payment gateways via Omnipay. The open-source core has 45,000+ GitHub stars and 400,000+ downloads; Aimeos GmbH offers professional support and works with a network of implementation partners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Aimeos is built for PHP developers and agencies already using Laravel, Symfony, or TYPO3 who need a fast, customizable commerce layer rather than an off-the-shelf storefront — including marketplace operators and B2B platforms needing multi-vendor or multi-warehouse support.

features.title: JSON REST & GraphQL APIs
features.description: API-first design for headless storefronts and integrations.

features.title: Multi-vendor, multi-channel, multi-warehouse
features.description: Native support for marketplace-style setups with multiple sellers and stock locations.

features.title: Large catalog scalability
features.description: Built to handle catalogs from 1 to roughly a billion items with fast page rendering.

features.title: Framework packages
features.description: Dedicated integration packages for Laravel, Symfony, and TYPO3.

features.title: Flexible product types
features.description: Supports bundles, subscriptions, and virtual products alongside standard items.

features.title: 100+ payment gateways
features.description: Payment integrations via the Omnipay library.

features.title: Multi-tenant capability
features.description: Can run multiple stores/tenants from a single installation for SaaS-style deployments.

pros: Free, open-source core with no licensing fees
Deep native integration with Laravel, Symfony, and TYPO3
Proven performance and scalability for large catalogs
Large community (45,000+ GitHub stars) and strong Capterra rating
cons: Requires PHP/framework development skills — not a no-code storefront builder
Professional support and some ecosystem extensions are paid
Less brand recognition among non-developers than platforms like Shopify

faqs.question: Is Aimeos free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Aimeos framework is free and open source; Aimeos GmbH separately offers paid professional support.

faqs.question: What PHP frameworks does Aimeos support?
faqs.answer: It ships with dedicated integration packages for Laravel, Symfony, and TYPO3.

faqs.question: Can Aimeos power a marketplace?
faqs.answer: Yes, it natively supports multiple vendors, sales channels, and warehouses.

faqs.question: Does Aimeos have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides both JSON REST and GraphQL APIs.

use_cases.title: Custom online shops
use_cases.description: Developers building a bespoke storefront on top of Laravel or Symfony.

use_cases.title: Marketplaces
use_cases.description: Multi-vendor marketplace platforms needing per-vendor catalogs and fulfillment.

use_cases.title: B2B commerce
use_cases.description: B2B platforms needing complex pricing, bundles, and subscription products.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Aimeos is a free, open-source PHP e-commerce framework for Laravel, Symfony, and TYPO3, built for large catalogs, marketplaces, and B2B commerce.

enrichment.short_description: Symfony-based e-commerce framework built on top of Pimcore, free for small businesses under an open-core commercial license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CoreShop is an e-commerce framework built as a set of Symfony bundles on top of Pimcore, the PHP product information management (PIM) and digital asset management (DAM) platform. It's built with Symfony and Doctrine ORM and is designed for deep customization of checkout, shipping, notifications, and localization.

CoreShop's source code is publicly available on GitHub, but as of its CoreShop Commercial License (CCL) it operates on an open-core model rather than a traditional open-source license: production use is free for organizations under roughly €5 million in annual revenue and for qualifying non-profits and educational institutions, while larger enterprises — including those on Pimcore Professional or Enterprise licenses — need a paid CCL license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CoreShop is aimed at businesses already using or planning to use Pimcore for product data management who want commerce functionality built directly on top of it, and at developers comfortable working in a Symfony/Doctrine codebase. Small businesses and non-profits under the revenue threshold can run it in production at no cost.

features.title: Symfony bundle architecture
features.description: Ships as modular Symfony bundles that plug into the full-stack framework.

features.title: Pimcore integration
features.description: Built directly on Pimcore's PIM/DAM data model for product-data-driven commerce.

features.title: Customizable checkout & shipping
features.description: Extendable notification, shipping, and checkout logic.

features.title: Pimcore Studio admin (React/TypeScript)
features.description: A rewritten admin interface with per-bundle Studio plugins using module federation.

features.title: Doctrine ORM data layer
features.description: Standard Symfony/Doctrine persistence layer for extending data models.

pros: Deep, native integration with Pimcore's product information management
Free in production for small businesses, non-profits, and qualifying education use
Highly customizable Symfony/Doctrine architecture
Actively developed on GitHub with commercial backing
cons: No longer a traditional open-source license — larger organizations need a paid CCL license
Requires Pimcore plus Symfony/PHP expertise to run
Licensing model is more complex than a straightforward free/open-source or SaaS pricing structure

faqs.question: Is CoreShop free?
faqs.answer: It's free for production use if your organization's annual revenue is under roughly €5 million, or if you qualify as a non-profit or educational organization; larger companies need a paid CCL license.

faqs.question: Is CoreShop open source?
faqs.answer: Its source code is publicly viewable on GitHub, but under the CoreShop Commercial License it is not licensed as traditional open-source software — it follows an open-core model.

faqs.question: Does CoreShop require Pimcore?
faqs.answer: Yes, CoreShop is built as a set of Symfony bundles on top of Pimcore's PIM/DAM platform.

faqs.question: Who is CoreShop best suited for?
faqs.answer: Organizations already invested in Pimcore for product data management that want e-commerce built on the same data model.

use_cases.title: Pimcore-based commerce
use_cases.description: Businesses already using Pimcore for PIM who want commerce on the same platform.

use_cases.title: Custom B2B/B2C shops
use_cases.description: Developers building highly customized storefronts on Symfony.

features: Production use for orgs under ~€5M annual revenue
Free for qualifying non-profits and education
Free for demos and proofs of concept
Required for larger enterprises and Pimcore Professional/Enterprise customers
Licensed use of the full CoreShop Suite

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CoreShop is a Symfony e-commerce framework built on Pimcore, free for small businesses and non-profits under its open-core CCL license model.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source TypeScript e-commerce platform built on Node.js, React, and GraphQL for developers who want full control over their store.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: EverShop is an open-source e-commerce platform built with TypeScript, Node.js/Express, React, and GraphQL, backed by PostgreSQL and styled with TailwindCSS/Sass. It's released under the GPL-3.0 license and hosted on GitHub, with a modular, fully customizable architecture aimed at developers rather than non-technical store owners.

It includes dynamic storefront widgets, product/category/collection management, and built-in support for Stripe and PayPal payments plus integrations such as Mailchimp and Algolia. A cloud-hosted version is listed as 'coming soon' on the official site, so today the platform is offered as free, self-hosted software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: EverShop targets JavaScript/TypeScript developers and agencies who want a modern, code-first, GraphQL-driven commerce backend they can fully customize, rather than a drag-and-drop or SaaS storefront builder.

features.title: GraphQL API
features.description: A GraphQL layer for querying storefront and catalog data.

features.title: React-based storefront widgets
features.description: Dynamic, customizable widgets for building storefront layouts.

features.title: Product & catalog management
features.description: Tools for managing products, categories, and collections.

features.title: Built-in payments
features.description: Native support for Stripe and PayPal checkout.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Integrates with services such as Mailchimp and Algolia.

features.title: Order tracking
features.description: Real-time order status and tracking for customers and staff.

pros: Modern TypeScript/Node.js/React stack familiar to web developers
Fully open source (GPL-3.0) with an active GitHub community
GraphQL-first architecture suited to headless or custom storefronts
No licensing fees for self-hosting
cons: GPL-3.0 copyleft license may restrict proprietary derivative products
Official cloud-hosted offering is not yet available
Younger project with a smaller plugin/theme ecosystem than established platforms

faqs.question: Is EverShop free?
faqs.answer: Yes, EverShop is free, open-source software licensed under GPL-3.0.

faqs.question: What tech stack does EverShop use?
faqs.answer: Node.js/Express and PostgreSQL on the backend, React and GraphQL on the frontend, styled with TailwindCSS/Sass.

faqs.question: Is there a hosted/cloud version?
faqs.answer: A cloud-hosted option is listed as 'coming soon' on the official EverShop site; currently it's offered as self-hosted software.

faqs.question: Does EverShop support Stripe and PayPal?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are supported as built-in payment providers.

use_cases.title: Custom headless storefronts
use_cases.description: Developers building a GraphQL-driven storefront with full control over the frontend.

use_cases.title: Agency client stores
use_cases.description: Agencies building customized Node.js-based stores for clients.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: EverShop is a free, open-source TypeScript e-commerce platform built on Node.js, React, and GraphQL for developer-driven online stores.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source drag-and-drop CMS and website builder with built-in e-commerce, free to self-host or available on paid cloud hosting plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microweber is an open-source drag-and-drop website builder and CMS that also includes e-commerce functionality, with over 100,000 installations worldwide. It ships with 450+ pre-built layouts and 75+ modules, and can be downloaded and self-hosted for free, or run through Microweber's own cloud-hosted plans.

The project is backed by the Microweber Academy Foundation, a non-profit public benefit organization registered in Bulgaria. Its site positions the product as an AI-assisted platform for building websites without coding knowledge, aimed particularly at web studios and marketing agencies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Microweber suits freelancers, agencies, and small businesses that want a no-code, drag-and-drop way to launch a website, blog, or online store — either self-hosted for free, or on a managed cloud plan for convenience and support.

features.title: Drag-and-drop builder
features.description: Visual, no-code page building interface.

features.title: 450+ layouts and 75+ modules
features.description: Large library of pre-built templates and modules to assemble pages.

features.title: Built-in CMS
features.description: Content management for pages, blogs, and site structure.

features.title: E-commerce
features.description: Built-in online store functionality alongside the CMS and builder.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Tools for building and managing sites in multiple languages.

features.title: AI-assisted site building
features.description: AI-based tooling positioned to help build functional websites without coding.

pros: Free to self-host with full source access
Drag-and-drop builder lowers the technical barrier to launching a site
Combines CMS, website builder, and e-commerce in one platform
Large install base (100,000+ installations)
cons: Higher-capacity features (more sites, storage, support) require paid cloud plans
Drag-and-drop builders can limit deep custom development compared to code-first frameworks
Documentation and marketing are geared more toward agencies/non-developers than engineers

faqs.question: Is Microweber free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source version is free to download and self-host. Microweber also sells separate cloud-hosted plans starting at $10/month.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Microweber?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source CMS/builder can be downloaded and self-hosted at no cost.

faqs.question: What do the paid cloud plans include?
faqs.answer: Cloud plans add managed hosting, more websites, storage, and support, ranging from a Personal plan up to Business and custom Enterprise tiers.

faqs.question: Who runs Microweber?
faqs.answer: The project is backed by the Microweber Academy Foundation, a non-profit registered in Bulgaria.

use_cases.title: Agency client sites
use_cases.description: Web studios and agencies building multiple client websites quickly.

use_cases.title: Small business websites and stores
use_cases.description: Non-technical business owners launching a site or online store.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microweber is an open-source drag-and-drop CMS, website builder, and e-commerce platform, free to self-host or available on paid cloud plans from $10/month.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source Laravel-based shopping cart and CMS platform for building online stores, part of the GP247 ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: s-cart is a free, open-source e-commerce platform built on the Laravel PHP framework as part of the GP247 ecosystem, licensed under MIT. It combines a shopping cart, checkout, and order management with a built-in CMS, and is maintained on GitHub under the GP247/s-cart organization.

The platform includes multi-language and multi-currency support, multi-vendor and multi-store capability, role-based admin permissions with security audit logging, payment and shipping plugins, and an API for mobile and third-party integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: s-cart is aimed at developers and small businesses who want a free, Laravel-native shopping cart they can self-host and customize, including those building multi-vendor or multi-store setups on a budget.

features.title: Laravel-based cart & checkout
features.description: Built on the Laravel framework with modern PHP and Tailwind CSS.

features.title: Built-in CMS
features.description: Content management for store pages alongside commerce features.

features.title: Multi-language & multi-currency
features.description: Supports selling in multiple languages and currencies.

features.title: Multi-vendor & multi-store
features.description: Can run multiple vendors or multiple storefronts from one installation.

features.title: Role-based admin & audit logging
features.description: Granular admin permissions with security audit logs.

features.title: API support
features.description: APIs for mobile apps and third-party integrations.

features.title: Discount & tax engine
features.description: Built-in tools for discounts and tax calculation.

pros: Fully free and open source under the MIT license
Built on the modern Laravel framework
Supports multi-vendor and multi-store setups out of the box
Active development on GitHub under the GP247 organization
cons: Smaller international community/ecosystem than platforms like Magento or WooCommerce
Documentation and community are less English-centric than larger Western projects
Requires PHP/Laravel hosting knowledge to deploy and maintain

faqs.question: Is s-cart free?
faqs.answer: Yes, s-cart is free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What framework is s-cart built on?
faqs.answer: It's built on the Laravel PHP framework as part of the GP247 ecosystem.

faqs.question: Can s-cart run multiple stores?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multi-vendor and multi-store configurations.

faqs.question: Where can I get the source code?
faqs.answer: The source is hosted on GitHub under the s-cart/GP247 organization.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source PHP/Symfony e-commerce platform used since 2005 by 1,500+ merchants, maintained by French company OpenStudio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Thelia is a French open-source e-commerce platform that has powered over 1,500 online stores in France and internationally since 2005. It's built on PHP and Symfony, using API Platform for interoperability with ERP, PIM, and CRM systems, MariaDB for storage, and Twig with Symfony UX for the frontend.

Development is stewarded by OpenStudio, a French software company with roughly 100 employees that holds ISO 9001 and Numérique Responsable certifications and is a member of French Tech. The project's source is hosted on GitHub, with 300+ available modules and support for headless/microservices architectures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Thelia is aimed at merchants and developers — especially in France and Europe — who want full infrastructure control and an API-first Symfony-based commerce platform that integrates cleanly with existing ERP, PIM, or CRM systems, rather than a hosted SaaS storefront.

features.title: Symfony-based architecture
features.description: Built on the Symfony framework for structured, standardized development.

features.title: API Platform integration
features.description: Interoperability with ERP, PIM, and CRM systems via API Platform.

features.title: 300+ modules
features.description: A module ecosystem for extending checkout, shipping, payments, and more.

features.title: Headless/microservices support
features.description: Can be run in headless or microservices-based architectures.

features.title: Twig + Symfony UX frontend
features.description: Modern templating and frontend interactivity via Symfony UX.

features.title: MariaDB data layer
features.description: Relational database backend for store and catalog data.

pros: Mature project with two decades of production use in French e-commerce
Backed by an established, certified company (OpenStudio) rather than a loose community
Fully open source and self-hostable with no vendor lock-in
API-first design for integrating with ERP/PIM/CRM systems
cons: Smaller international/English-language community than platforms like Magento or PrestaShop
Primarily French-market focused in its community and module ecosystem
Requires Symfony/PHP development expertise to run and extend

faqs.question: Is Thelia free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Thelia is open-source software; the code is hosted publicly on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who maintains Thelia?
faqs.answer: OpenStudio, a French software company with about 100 employees, stewards Thelia's development.

faqs.question: What framework is Thelia built on?
faqs.answer: PHP and Symfony, with API Platform, MariaDB, and Twig/Symfony UX.

faqs.question: Does Thelia support headless commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports headless and microservices-based architectures.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, Google Reader-inspired RSS feed reader that can be self-hosted or used free at commafeed.com.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CommaFeed is a free, open-source RSS feed reader inspired by Google Reader, built with a Java/Quarkus backend and a React/TypeScript frontend, and released under the Apache-2.0 license. It can be self-hosted via official Docker images or used through the free public instance at commafeed.com, which the maintainers say has no ads or tracking and is funded entirely by donations.

It offers four customizable layouts, light/dark themes, keyboard shortcuts, OPML import/export, a REST API, a Fever-compatible API for use with existing RSS client apps, automatic read rules, push notifications, and a browser extension. The project is designed to scale to thousands of users and millions of feeds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CommaFeed suits people who want a Google Reader-style RSS reading experience — either self-hosted for full control and privacy, or via the free hosted instance — as well as developers who want to build custom RSS clients against its REST or Fever-compatible API.

features.title: Self-hosted via Docker
features.description: Official Docker images make self-hosting straightforward.

features.title: Free public instance
features.description: A free, ad-free, donation-funded hosted instance at commafeed.com.

features.title: REST & Fever-compatible API
features.description: Lets third-party RSS client apps connect to a CommaFeed instance.

features.title: OPML import/export
features.description: Move subscriptions in and out of CommaFeed easily.

features.title: Multiple layouts & themes
features.description: Four layout options plus light and dark themes.

features.title: Automatic read rules & push notifications
features.description: Rule-based automatic marking of items as read, plus push notifications for new content.

features.title: Browser extension
features.description: A companion browser extension for quick subscribing and reading.

pros: Completely free — self-hosted or on the hosted instance, with no paid tier
Apache-2.0 open source with active GitHub development
Fever API compatibility works with many existing third-party RSS apps
The public hosted instance is funded by donations, not ads or data sales
cons: No official first-party mobile app — relies on the browser or Fever-compatible third-party clients
Feature set is simpler than some modern read-it-later/RSS tools
Self-hosting means running a Java/Quarkus stack and database

faqs.question: Is CommaFeed free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's completely free — both to self-host and to use on the public commafeed.com instance.

faqs.question: Can I self-host CommaFeed?
faqs.answer: Yes, official Docker images make self-hosting straightforward.

faqs.question: Does CommaFeed have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There's no official first-party mobile app; users typically rely on the responsive web interface or third-party Fever-compatible apps.

faqs.question: What is the Fever API used for?
faqs.answer: It lets CommaFeed work as a backend for existing RSS client apps that support the Fever API standard.

use_cases.title: Personal RSS reading
use_cases.description: Individuals wanting a Google Reader-style feed reader.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused self-hosting
use_cases.description: Users who want to keep their reading data under their own control.

use_cases.title: Custom RSS client development
use_cases.description: Developers building their own RSS client against CommaFeed's REST/Fever API.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CommaFeed is a free, open-source, Google Reader-inspired RSS reader you can self-host via Docker or use free at commafeed.com.
